references.citation_raw: Bloch, E. (1986). The Principle of Hope (3 vols., trans. N. Plaice, S. Plaice & P. Knight). MIT Press. (Original work published 1954–1959)

applied_codes.justification: Ernst Bloch frames human aspiration toward a better future as a product of 'anticipatory consciousness' that looks beyond current limitations and alienation.
applied_codes.verbatim_evidence: arguing that humanity harbors an anticipatory consciousness of a more perfect future.

applied_codes.justification: Bloch introduces a unique epistemic mode that synthesizes the objective, socio-economic 'cold stream' of analysis with the subjective, imaginative 'warm stream' of hope, giving rise to 'docta spes' (comprehended hope).
applied_codes.verbatim_evidence: He synthesizes the 'cold stream' of Marxist social and economic analysis with the 'warm stream' of forward-dreaming utopian speculation to define 'concrete utopia'—a vision rooted in material drives to overcome alienation

applied_codes.justification: The functional purpose of Bloch's speculative philosophy is active transformation. He positions concrete utopia not as passive speculation, but as a practice of changing the world through Marxist framework.
applied_codes.verbatim_evidence: This road is and remains that of socialism, it is the practice of concrete utopia. Everything that is non-illusory, real-possible about the hope-images leads to Marx, works – as always, in different ways, rationed according to the situation – as part of socialist changing of the world.

references.citation_raw: Booker, M.K. (1994). The Dystopian Impulse in Modern Literature: Fiction as Social Criticism. Greenwood Press.

applied_codes.justification: The text frames dystopian literature not as literal prediction, but as a cautionary, warning-oriented forecast designed to highlight modern institutional and political risks by exaggerating contemporary trends.
applied_codes.verbatim_evidence: Dystopian literature is not a depiction of what will be, but a warning of what might be if contemporary trends continue.

applied_codes.justification: The theoretical methodology utilized by Booker relies on classical philosophical and psychoanalytic frameworks (Nietzsche and Freud) to analyze the systemic pathologies, anxieties, and power structures of modern societies.
applied_codes.verbatim_evidence: Drawing upon the theoretical works of Friedrich Nietzsche and Sigmund Freud, Booker contrasts the optimism of utopian literature with the critical warning systems of dystopias...

applied_codes.justification: The functional purpose identified for speculative/dystopian narratives is to defamiliarize real-world practices and institutions to foster critical reflection and social critique, rather than proposing a blueprint for direct institutional replacement.
applied_codes.verbatim_evidence: By defamiliarizing the familiar, it allows us to see our own social and political practices with fresh, critical eyes, serving as an indispensable mode of social criticism.

references.citation_raw: Claeys, G. (2017). Dystopia: A Natural History. Oxford University Press.

applied_codes.justification: The text adopts a cautionary anticipatory stance, warning that modern scientific and technological advancements carry profound, latent destructive capabilities that offset their apparent benefits.
applied_codes.verbatim_evidence: Fear of the science which takes away with one hand even more than what it so profusely gives with the other.

applied_codes.justification: The text relies on an epistemic framework that bridges fictional speculation with real-world history, evaluating modern technological anxieties through the lens of empirical historical collapses and historical despotisms.
applied_codes.verbatim_evidence: He broadens the concept of dystopia beyond literary totalitarians to encompass real-world despotisms and societies experiencing collapse.

applied_codes.justification: The functional intent of analyzing dystopian evolution is to construct an intellectual critique of modernity's crises, interrogating societal structures by tracing how fear is mobilized historically.
applied_codes.verbatim_evidence: traces the chronological evolution of dystopian thought from its early nineteenth-century literary origins to the modern era, viewing dystopia as a response to the crisis of modernity.
